{"key_event":"1988 northern war and expanding clan insurgencies","reasoning":"Barre’s regime retained significant coercive capacity through security organs and foreign aid, but fiscal decay, clanization, insurgent expansion, and loss of control in the north reduced it from a strong late-1970s state to a contested, weakening one. Society’s formal civic space was brutally repressed, yet clan, diaspora, market, and armed opposition networks gained enough mobilizing capacity to push civil society power modestly upward in fragmented form.","society_change":0.7,"society_power":4.5,"state_change":-2.1,"state_power":4.8}
